_comment: Per-chart narratives. CI (build_prompt.py -> GitHub Models -> write_narrative.py) overwrites these from live numbers; this committed copy is the technical fallback so the page always reads well. source = 'fallback' | 'github-models'.

notes.cape: The Shiller CAPE ratio (Cyclically Adjusted Price-to-Earnings) divides the current price of the S&P 500 by the average real earnings of the index over the past ten years, smoothing cyclical volatility to gauge market valuation. As of June 2026, the CAPE stands at 40.7, substantially above its 140-year average of 17.3, and near its only-ever higher peak of 44.19 in December 1999. Historically, CAPE levels above approximately 39 have signaled weaker forward returns, with the S&P 500 averaging around -4% over the next year and -20% over two years, reflecting profit margin concerns and stretched valuations. However, elevated CAPE readings do not precisely time market declines; extreme valuations highlight vulnerability but do not indicate when the liquidity underpinning the rally will evaporate.
notes.buffett: The Buffett Indicator is the ratio of total U.S. equity market capitalization to nominal GDP, measuring market value relative to economic output. At 231.7% in June 2026, it far exceeds past peaks of 146% at the 2000 dot-com bubble and 110% before the 2007 financial crisis, underscoring extreme overvaluation. Warren Buffett has cautioned that readings over 200% represent "playing with fire," as markets become disconnected from underlying economic fundamentals, increasing systemic risk. Nonetheless, the ratio does not offer timing signals; extended market capitalization may persist if liquidity remains ample, delaying correction despite valuation distortion.
notes.concentration: Concentration metrics quantify the share of total market capitalization held by top companies or sectors, indicating risk of narrow driver dependencies. Currently, the top 5 stocks constitute approximately 29.8% of the S&P 500, and the tech sector accounts for an unusually high 48%. The top 10 stocks weigh 41.0% in 2026, more than double the 17.5% share observed in 2015, reflecting heightened concentration and potential fragility. Such concentration can amplify drawdowns if key leaders stumble, as broad market performance becomes contingent on few large-cap performers. However, concentration alone does not predict market timing; it highlights heightened vulnerability if external shocks erode liquidity or sentiment.
notes.yieldcurve: The yield curve spread between the 10-year Treasury and 3-month T-bill rates is a classic indicator of monetary conditions and recession risk. A positive 0.55 percentage points spread as of June 2026 indicates a nominally upward sloping curve, but well below historical averages, suggesting market anticipation of slower growth. The New York Fed’s recession probability model quantifies a 31% chance of economic contraction within 12 months, implying elevated risk. The yield curve matters as inversion or flattening typically signals tighter financial conditions and reduced liquidity, often leading to market stress. However, yield curve signals are imperfect timing tools and can be influenced by technical and policy factors unrelated to fundamental credit risk.
notes.drypowder: Dry powder refers to the cash reserves investors hold, available for opportunistic deployment during market dislocations. Berkshire Hathaway’s record $397.4 billion cash position in Q1 2026 reflects unprecedented institutional liquidity reserves, signaling capacity to buy assets at depressed levels. Despite this, 79% of institutional investors expect their overall dry powder to decline, suggesting potential forthcoming reductions in liquidity buffering the market. Large cash holdings can delay or mitigate downturns by providing buying support when "the music stops," but the eventual reduction in available dry powder may coincide with amplified downside. Predicting the timing of liquidity shifts remains elusive since dry powder levels respond to market sentiment and fundraising dynamics.
notes.finale: Valuation metrics such as CAPE and the Buffett Indicator quantify how far prices have stretched relative to earnings and economic output, setting the potential magnitude of any correction but not pinpointing its onset. Market timing hinges on liquidity shocks—the sudden evaporation of risk appetite or capital inflows—that abruptly halt the momentum keeping investors "dancing." The anticipated trigger window between December 2026 and May 2027 aligns with IPO lock-up expirations for major companies like SpaceX, OpenAI, and Anthropic, a period historically associated with increased selling pressure and liquidity tightening. This convergence of extreme valuation, concentrated leadership, reduced dry powder expectations, and a modestly positive but vulnerable yield curve underscores the precariousness of current market conditions, highlighting that while "the music" has been playing to record highs, timing the stop remains uncertain.
